features.summary: NVIDIA reported Q3 revenue of $5.93 billion, down 12% sequentially and 17% year-on-year, with record data center and automotive revenue. Data center revenue was $3.83 billion, up 1% sequentially and 31% year-on-year, driven by demand from U.S. cloud providers and other industries. Gaming revenue fell to $1.57 billion, down 23% sequentially and 51% year-on-year, while pro visualization revenue dropped to $200 million, down 60% sequentially and 65% year-on-year. Automotive revenue increased to $251 million, up 14% sequentially and 86% year-on-year.
features.management_outlook: For Q4, NVIDIA expects modest sequential growth with revenue projected at $6 billion, plus or minus 2%. Data center revenue is anticipated to reflect early shipments of the A100, while gaming is expected to resume growth as channel inventory normalizes. Automotive is expected to continue its ramp with Oren design wins, contributing to overall growth.
features.risks: NVIDIA faces risks from ongoing softness in demand in China, particularly affecting data center revenue. New U.S. export restrictions on certain products to China may also impact future sales. Additionally, the company is navigating inventory corrections in its gaming and pro visualization segments, which could affect revenue stability in the near term.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers include the increasing adoption of AI across various sectors, particularly in data centers and automotive applications. The launch of new products, such as the H-100 GPU and the Ada Lovelace architecture, is expected to enhance performance and efficiency, driving demand. Partnerships with major cloud providers like Microsoft and Oracle are also pivotal in expanding NVIDIA's market reach and software monetization.
